skill.name: relight
skill.description: Relight a still image — change the lighting setup, color temperature, direction, or mood — on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. Routes to Qwen Edit 2509's dedicated `relight` LoRA endpoint for purpose-built relighting, with fallback to identity-preserving edit endpoints (Nano Banana 2 Edit, GPT Image 2 Edit, FLUX Kontext Pro) when prose lighting language is enough. Use for product relighting (studio softbox → window light), portrait mood shift (overcast → golden hour), or color-grade change. Triggers on "relight", "relighting", "change the lighting", "make it golden hour", "studio lighting", "rim light", "blue hour", "soft window light", "change light direction", "color temperature", or any explicit ask to alter how a still is lit.
skill.summary: Relight a still image — change the lighting setup, color temperature, direction, or mood — on RunComfy via the runcomfy CLI
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 93 potential issues, but all are false positives. The scanner misidentified markdown inline code formatting as Ruby backtick execution, and flagged image editing endpoint names (Edit) as weak cryptographic algorithms. The skill is a legitimate image relighting interface that calls the runcomfy CLI with hardcoded model paths. Tool scope is properly constrained to Bash(runcomfy *), and input is passed via JSON without shell expansion.

medium_findings.title: Markdown Inline Code Misidentified as Shell Execution
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged backtick characters in markdown as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are actually markdown inline code formatting (e.g., `runcomfy`, `--input`) used for documentation, not code execution. The skill only uses Bash(runcomfy *) with hardcoded command paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Backticks in SKILL.md are markdown syntax for inline code formatting, not Ruby shell execution. Evidence: allowed-tools specifies Bash(runcomfy *) only, and the skill only makes authenticated API calls to runcomfy endpoints.

low_findings.title: Image Editing Endpoints Misidentified as Cryptographic Algorithms
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'Edit' in endpoint names as weak cryptographic algorithms. These are image editing model endpoints (Qwen Edit Relight, Nano Banana 2 Edit, etc.), not cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The word 'Edit' appears in image editing model names like 'Qwen Edit Relight' and 'Nano Banana 2 Edit'. This is standard naming for image editing endpoints, not cryptographic algorithm references.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Misidentified as Network Exfiltration
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged hardcoded URLs as potential network exfiltration. These are legitimate documentation links to runcomfy.com services that users need to access the skill functionality.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs are static documentation links to runcomfy.com services. The skill Security section explicitly states only model-api.runcomfy.net and *.runcomfy.net/* are used for outbound connections.

low_findings.title: Standard Token Storage Misidentified as Hidden File Access
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json as hidden file access. This is standard token storage for the runcomfy CLI, following common conventions like ~/.config.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json is the standard configuration directory for CLI token storage. This follows common conventions and is explicitly documented as a security measure with mode 0600 permissions.

content.user_title: Relight images with precise lighting control
content.value_statement: Change the lighting direction, color temperature, and mood of any still image without redoing the shot. This skill routes to purpose-built AI relighting models for professional results.
actual_capabilities: Relight product photos with studio softbox, window light, or natural lighting setups
Change portrait mood from overcast to golden hour or blue hour aesthetics
Adjust color temperature from warm to cool tones across entire images
Apply multi-light setups with key, fill, and rim lighting from specified directions
Preserve subject identity, pose, and framing during lighting changes
Match lighting from reference photos to target images
limitations: Cannot generate images from scratch — only modifies existing images
Cannot relight videos — only works with still images
CLI login required — users need a runcomfy account and API token

use_cases.title: Product photography relighting
use_cases.description: E-commerce teams can relight product catalog images from harsh studio lighting to soft window light for lifestyle aesthetics without reshooting.
use_cases.target_user: E-commerce teams, product photographers, catalog managers

use_cases.title: Portrait mood enhancement
use_cases.description: Portrait photographers can shift mood from flat overcast lighting to dramatic golden hour or add rim lighting for depth, preserving subject identity.
use_cases.target_user: Portrait photographers, portrait retouchers, social media managers

use_cases.title: Batch lighting consistency
use_cases.description: Marketing teams can apply consistent lighting across entire product galleries to match brand aesthetics or seasonal campaigns.
use_cases.target_user: Marketing teams, brand managers, creative agencies

prompt_templates.title: Basic relight request
prompt_templates.prompt: Relight this product photo with soft window light from the left, late-afternoon warm temperature. Preserve the product orientation and color.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have a product image that needs softer, more natural lighting

prompt_templates.title: Portrait mood shift
prompt_templates.prompt: Change the lighting on this portrait to golden hour with warm key light from camera-left at 45 degrees, soft fill from right, no rim light. Preserve the subject's identity.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you want to transform a portrait from flat lighting to warm, dramatic lighting

prompt_templates.title: Multi-light studio setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Relight this product as a studio setup: warm 3200K key light from camera-left at 45 degrees, soft cool fill from right, hair rim from behind. Preserve product orientation and color identity.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need precise multi-light control for professional studio product shots

prompt_templates.title: Reference photo lighting match
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the lighting from this reference photo to the main image. Match the direction, color temperature, and contrast. Preserve the main image's subject identity.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have a reference photo with ideal lighting and want to apply that same lighting to another image

output_examples.input: A product photo lit with harsh overhead fluorescent lighting
output_examples.output: The same product relit with soft window light from the left, warm late-afternoon color temperature, subtle shadows adding depth

output_examples.input: A portrait with flat overcast lighting
output_examples.output: The same portrait with golden hour rim lighting from behind, warm soft key light from front-left, dramatic mood while preserving subject identity

output_examples.input: A landscape photo at high noon with harsh shadows
output_examples.output: The same landscape transformed to blue hour with soft, even lighting and cool color temperature

best_practices: Lead with lighting type then quantify: specify light source, color temperature, direction, and intensity separately for best results
State preservation explicitly: include phrases like 'preserve subject pose, framing, and color identity' to prevent model drift
Use time-of-day shortcuts: 'golden hour', 'blue hour', 'high-noon', and 'overcast' resolve to correct color temperature and softness automatically
anti_patterns: Do not ask for both relighting and major content changes in the same prompt — split into separate passes for best lighting fidelity
Do not skip preservation language for portraits or product shots — the model may drift without explicit identity preservation instructions
Do not use generic edit endpoints when you need precise lighting control — use Qwen Edit Relight LoRA for dedicated relighting work

faq.question: What is the difference between Qwen Edit Relight and Nano Banana 2 Edit?
faq.answer: Qwen Edit Relight uses a purpose-built relighting LoRA for maximum lighting fidelity. Nano Banana 2 Edit is better when relighting is part of a broader edit that also changes background or adds objects.

faq.question: Can I relight videos with this skill?
faq.answer: No, this skill only works with still images. For video relighting, RunComfy offers ComfyUI workflows with IC-Light variants.

faq.question: Do I need a RunComfy account to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, you need to sign in with 'runcomfy login' or set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable. The CLI is free to install but requires API authentication.

faq.question: How do I preserve the subject identity during relighting?
faq.answer: Include explicit preservation language in your prompt: 'preserve subject pose, framing, and color identity'. This prevents the model from drifting during lighting changes.

faq.question: What lighting parameters can I control?
faq.answer: You can control light source type, color temperature (warm/cool in Kelvin), direction (camera-left, top-down, rim from behind), intensity (soft/hard), and overall mood.

faq.question: Can I apply the same lighting to multiple images?
faq.answer: Yes, use Nano Banana 2 Edit with an image_urls array and the same lighting prompt across the batch for consistent lighting across product galleries.
